subject: Physics
q: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency <math><mn>50 </mn><mi></mi><mi>M</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi></math> travels in free space along the positive <math><mi>x</mi></math> - direction. At a particular point in space and time, <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>6.3</mn><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow></mrow></math> The corresponding magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> at that point will be:
question: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency <math><mn>50 </mn><mi></mi><mi>M</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi></math> travels in free space along the positive <math><mi>x</mi></math> - direction. At a particular point in space and time, <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>6.3</mn><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow></mrow></math> The corresponding magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> at that point will be:
solution:
As we know EB=C

E=6.3V/m

B=EC=6.33×108T=2.1×10-8T

q: If Electric field intensity of a uniform plane electro magnetic wave is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>301</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi>x</mi></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>452</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi>y</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Then, magnetic intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> of this wave in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be [Given : Speed of light in vacuum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, Permeability of vacuum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: If Electric field intensity of a uniform plane electro magnetic wave is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>301</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi>x</mi></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>452</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi>y</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Then, magnetic intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> of this wave in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be [Given : Speed of light in vacuum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, Permeability of vacuum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
The relation between magnetic field and electric field is given by,

c=E0B0⇒B0=E0c

And magnetic intensity is given by,

H0=B0μ0⇒H0=E0cμ0

Also, E→ and B→ are perpendicular to each other.

Therefore,

Hy0=301.63×108×4π×10-7=0.8 A m-1Hx0=452.43×108×4π×10-7=1.2 A m-1

Hence, C is the answer.

q:
The electric field and magnetic field components of an electromagnetic wave going through vacuum is described by

 Ex=E0sinkz-ωt

By=B0sinkz-ωt

Then the correct relation between E0 and B0 is given by
question:
The electric field and magnetic field components of an electromagnetic wave going through vacuum is described by

 Ex=E0sinkz-ωt

By=B0sinkz-ωt

Then the correct relation between E0 and B0 is given by
solution:
As we know, electric field and magnetic field are related as 

E0=cB0, where, c is speed of light in vacuum

Also c=ωk

⇒E0=ωkB0

⇒  E0k=ωB0

subject: Physics
q:
A beam of light travelling along X-axis is described by the electric field Ey=900sinωt-xc. The ratio of electric force to magnetic force on a charge q moving along Y-axis with a speed of 3×107 m s-1 will be :

[Given speed of light =3×108 m s-1 ]
question:
A beam of light travelling along X-axis is described by the electric field Ey=900sinωt-xc. The ratio of electric force to magnetic force on a charge q moving along Y-axis with a speed of 3×107 m s-1 will be :

[Given speed of light =3×108 m s-1 ]
solution:
Given, Ey=900sinωt-ωxc

Here, E0=900

Electric field and electric force are related as FE=qE0

Magnetic force of a moving charge is given by FB=qvB0

Thus, the ratio FEFB=E0vB0

Using relation between magnetic and electric field, we have

FEFB=cv=3×1083×107=10:1

q: The mean intensity of radiation on the surface of the Sun is about <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>W</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow><mo>.</mo></math> The rms value of the corresponding magnetic field is closest to:
question: The mean intensity of radiation on the surface of the Sun is about <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>W</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow><mo>.</mo></math> The rms value of the corresponding magnetic field is closest to:
solution:
I=B0 22μ0×C

B0=2μ0IC

Brms=μ0IC=4π×10-7×1083×108

≈10-4 T

subject: Physics
q: A displacement current of $4.0$ A can be set up in the space between two parallel plates of $6$ $\mu$F capacitor. The rate of change of potential difference across the plates of the capacitor is nearly $\alpha \times 10^6$ V/s. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
question: A displacement current of $4.0$ A can be set up in the space between two parallel plates of $6$ $\mu$F capacitor. The rate of change of potential difference across the plates of the capacitor is nearly $\alpha \times 10^6$ V/s. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
solution:
The displacement current $I_d$ between the plates of a capacitor is given by the formula:

$I_d = C \dfrac{dV}{dt}$

Substituting the given values of displacement current and capacitance:

$4.0 = 6 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{dV}{dt}$

$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{4.0}{6 \times 10^{-6}}$

$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 10^6 \approx 0.67 \times 10^6$ V/s

Comparing this with the given rate of change of potential difference $\alpha \times 10^6$ V/s, we get $\alpha = 0.67$.

Answer: $0.67$

subject: Physics
q:
The electric field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by :
$E_{y}=69 \sin \left[0.6 \times 10^{3} x-1.8 \times 10^{11} t\right] \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$.
The expression for magnetic field associated with this electromagnetic wave is $\_\_\_\_$ T.
question:
The electric field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by :
$E_{y}=69 \sin \left[0.6 \times 10^{3} x-1.8 \times 10^{11} t\right] \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$.
The expression for magnetic field associated with this electromagnetic wave is $\_\_\_\_$ T.
solution:
Given $E_y = 69 \sin[0.6 \times 10^3 x - 1.8 \times 10^{11} t]$ V/m
Wave propagates along +x (form $kx - \omega t$), $\vec{E}$ is along y.
For EM wave: $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$ = direction of propagation
$\hat{j} \times \hat{k} = \hat{i}$, so $\vec{B}$ is along z-direction.
$B_0 = \frac{E_0}{c} = \frac{69}{3 \times 10^8} = 2.3 \times 10^{-7}$ T
E and B are in phase for plane EM wave.
$B_z = 2.3 \times 10^{-7} \sin[0.6 \times 10^3 x - 1.8 \times 10^{11} t]$ T

Assertion (A) : Electromagnetic waves carry energy but not momentum.
Reason (\(\mathbf{R}\)): Mass of a photon is zero.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Electromagnetic waves carry energy but not momentum.
Reason (\(\mathbf{R}\)): Mass of a photon is zero.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of \((\mathbf{A})\)
Both \((\mathbf{A})\) and \((\mathbf{R})\) are true but \((\mathbf{R})\) is not the correct explanation of \((\mathbf{A})\)
solution: EM wave carry both energy and momentum. Rest mass of photon is zero.

subject: Physics
q:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave travelling through a medium is given by
$\vec{E}(x, t)=25 \sin \left(2.0 \times 10^{15} t-10^{7} x\right) \hat{n}$
then the refractive index of the medium is $\_\_\_\_$.
(All given measurement are in SI units)
question:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave travelling through a medium is given by
$\vec{E}(x, t)=25 \sin \left(2.0 \times 10^{15} t-10^{7} x\right) \hat{n}$
then the refractive index of the medium is $\_\_\_\_$.
(All given measurement are in SI units)
solution:
The electric field is $E(x,t) = 25\sin(2.0 \times 10^{15}t - 10^7 x)$ V/m.
This is in the standard form $E = E_0\sin(\omega t - kx)$ where angular frequency $\omega = 2.0 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s and wave number $k = 10^7$ rad/m.
The speed of the wave in the medium is $v = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{15}}{10^7} = 2.0 \times 10^8$ m/s.
The refractive index is defined as $n = \frac{c}{v}$ where $c = 3.0 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light in vacuum.
Therefore, $n = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8}{2.0 \times 10^8} = 1.5$

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor of area $A=16 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ and separation between the plates 10 cm , is charged by a DC current. Consider a hypothetical plane surface of area $\mathrm{A}_0=3.2 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ inside the capacitor and parallel to the plates. At an instant, the current through the circuit is 6A. At the same instant the displacement current through $\mathrm{A}_0$ is ________ mA .
question: A parallel plate capacitor of area $A=16 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ and separation between the plates 10 cm , is charged by a DC current. Consider a hypothetical plane surface of area $\mathrm{A}_0=3.2 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ inside the capacitor and parallel to the plates. At an instant, the current through the circuit is 6A. At the same instant the displacement current through $\mathrm{A}_0$ is ________ mA .
solution:
$i_d=i_c$
$\therefore$ Total displacement current $=6 \mathrm{~A}$
Through $A_0$
$\begin{aligned}
& i=\left(\frac{A_0}{A}\right) 6 \\
& =\frac{3.2}{16} \times 6=1.2 \mathrm{~A}=1200 \mathrm{~mA}
\end{aligned}$

q: An electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>GHz</mi></math> enters a dielectric medium of relative electric permittivity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn></math> from vacuum. The wavelength of this wave in that medium will be _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>GHz</mi></math> enters a dielectric medium of relative electric permittivity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn></math> from vacuum. The wavelength of this wave in that medium will be _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
λ in vacuum =cf=3×1083×109=0.1 m

∴  λ in medium =0.1μ

Where refractive index

μ=μrεr

Assuming non-magnetic material μr=1

∴  μ=2.25=1.5

λm=0.11.5=115 m=6.67 cm

=667×10-2 cm

Choose the correct option.

options: The frequency of electromagnetic wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>54</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>.

The transmitted wave will be
3.1cos1.8z-5.4×106ti^ N/C.

The reflected wave will be
3.1cos1.8z+5.4×106ti^ N C-1.
solution: Reflected wave will have direction opposite to incident wave.

subject: Physics
q: The electromagnetic waves travel in a medium at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The relative permeability of the medium is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. The relative permittivity of the medium will be
question: The electromagnetic waves travel in a medium at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The relative permeability of the medium is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. The relative permittivity of the medium will be
solution:
Here, v=2×108 m s−1 and μr=1

The speed of electromagnetic waves in a medium is given by v=1με  ...i

where μ and ε are absolute permeability and absolute permittivity of the medium.
Now, μ=μ0μr and ε=ε0εr
Then, we have, 

v=1μ0μr×ε0εr=1μ0ε0×1μrεr=cμrεr

orεr=c2v2μr=3×10822×1082=94=2.25

q: Which is the correct ascending order of wavelengths?
question: Which is the correct ascending order of wavelengths?
solution:
We know that E=hcλ⇒E∝1λ. Also,

Egamma-ray>EX-ray>Evisible>Emicrowave

Therefore,

λgamma-ray<λX-ray<λvisible<λmicrowave

What will be the peak magnetic field at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> distance produced by the radiations coming from this bulb? Consider this bulb as a point source with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> efficiency.
question: An electric bulb is rated as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. What will be the peak magnetic field at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> distance produced by the radiations coming from this bulb? Consider this bulb as a point source with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> efficiency.
solution:
Intensity of light is I=PowerArea=200×3.51004π42=0.034 W m-2

Using the relation between magnetic field and intensity, I=B02c2μ0

We have, magnetic field as B0=I×2μ0c=0.034×2×4π×10-73×108=1.69×10-8 T≈1.71×10-8 T

subject: Physics
q: A plane electromagnetic wave is moving in free space with velocity $c=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and its electric field is given as $\vec{E}=54 \sin (k z-\omega t) \hat{j} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$, where $\hat{j}$ is the unit vector along $y$-axis. The magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ of the wave is:
question: A plane electromagnetic wave is moving in free space with velocity $c=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and its electric field is given as $\vec{E}=54 \sin (k z-\omega t) \hat{j} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$, where $\hat{j}$ is the unit vector along $y$-axis. The magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ of the wave is:
solution:
For an electromagnetic wave, the relationship between electric and magnetic field amplitudes is $B_0 = \frac{E_0}{c}$.
Here $E_0 = 54$ V/m and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
So, $B_0 = \frac{54}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.8 \times 10^{-7}$ T.
The electric field is along the $\hat{j}$ direction.
Since the wave travels in the $+z$ direction, the magnetic field direction is determined by $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$ pointing in the $+z$ direction.
Using the right-hand rule: $\hat{j} \times (-\hat{i}) = \hat{k}$ (positive $z$ direction), confirming the magnetic field is in the $-\hat{i}$ direction.
Therefore, $\vec{B} = -1.8 \times 10^{-7}\sin(kz - \omega t)\hat{i}$ T.

subject: Physics
q: All electromagnetic wave is transporting energy in the negative <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> direction. At a certain point and certain time the direction of electric field of the wave is along positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> direction. What will be the direction of the magnetic field of the wave at that point and instant?
question: All electromagnetic wave is transporting energy in the negative <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> direction. At a certain point and certain time the direction of electric field of the wave is along positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> direction. What will be the direction of the magnetic field of the wave at that point and instant?
solution:
In an electromagnetic wave, the direction of energy flow is along the direction of the wave propagation. Therefore, the wave propagates along negative z direction. 

Electric field is along the positive y direction and the wave propagates along negative z direction. Thus, magnetic field is in direction perpendicular to both z and y axes. 

Using vector algebra, 

(-k^)=(+j^)×i^

Hence, magnetic field is in positive x direction.

solution:
B=Ec=603×108=2×10-7
The magnetic field can be written as,
k=2πλ=2πc/f≈500
ω=2πf≈1.5×1011
B→=2×10-7sin⁡(0.5×103z-1.5×1011t)i^

subject: Physics
q: A red LED emits light at 0.1 watt uniformly around it. The amplitude of the electric field of the light at a distance of 1 m from the diode is:
question: A red LED emits light at 0.1 watt uniformly around it. The amplitude of the electric field of the light at a distance of 1 m from the diode is:
solution:
For a point source of power = P, then intensity at a point at a separation x from the source is, I=PowerArea=P4πx2∵ The average intensity of E.M. wave is given by, I=12cε0Eo2⇒E0=2P4πε0c x2

∵14πε0=9×109, P=0.1 W, x=1 m, c=3×108 m s-1

⇒E0=2×0.1×9×1093×108×12=6=2.45 V m-1

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave of frequency $1 \times 10^{14}$ hertz is propagating along $\mathrm{z}$-axis. The amplitude of electric field is $4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$. If $\varepsilon_0=8.8 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 / \mathrm{N}-$ $\mathrm{m}^2$, then average energy density of electric field will be:
question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency $1 \times 10^{14}$ hertz is propagating along $\mathrm{z}$-axis. The amplitude of electric field is $4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$. If $\varepsilon_0=8.8 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 / \mathrm{N}-$ $\mathrm{m}^2$, then average energy density of electric field will be:
solution:
Given: Amplitude of electric field,
$$
E_0=4 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{m}
$$
Absolute permitivity,
$$
\varepsilon_0=8.8 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{c}^2 / \mathrm{N}-\mathrm{m}^2
$$
Average energy density $u_E=$ ?
Applying formula,
Average energy density $u_E=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_0 E^2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\Rightarrow \quad u_E=\frac{1}{4} \times 8.8 \times 10^{-12} \times(4)^2 \\
&=35.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

q: The electric field of a plane polarized electromagnetic wave in free space at time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is given by the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>z</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is given by (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is the velocity of light.)
question: The electric field of a plane polarized electromagnetic wave in free space at time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is given by the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>z</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is given by (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is the velocity of light.)
solution:
E=E0cosωt-K  →· r →

∴ At any time t,

K→=6i^+8k^

B0=E0c=10c

λ=2πk=2π10=π5

Also, E→×B→=c→

∴ω=2πf=2πcλ

i^j^k^010BxByBz=6i^+8k^10

ω=2π×cπ5=10c

∴i^Bz-0+j^0+k^-Bx=0.6i^-0.8k^

Comparing the coefficients on both sides,

∴Bz=0.6; Bx=-0.8

B0→=10c-0.8i^+0.6k^=-8i^+6k^c

So the magnetic field,B→=1c-8i^+6k^cos⁡6x+8z-10ct tesla

=9NC
So,

subject: Physics
q:
A laser beam has intensity of $4.0 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The amplitude of magnetic field associated with beam is $\_\_\_\_$ T.
(Take $\epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} / \mathrm{Nm}^{2}$ and $\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$)
question:
A laser beam has intensity of $4.0 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The amplitude of magnetic field associated with beam is $\_\_\_\_$ T.
(Take $\epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} / \mathrm{Nm}^{2}$ and $\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$)
solution:
The intensity of an electromagnetic wave is
$I = \frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 c E_0^2$.
From the relation $E_0 = c B_0$, we get $I = \frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 c^3 B_0^2$.
Solving for magnetic field amplitude:
$B_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2I}{\epsilon_0 c^3}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{14}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times (3 \times 10^8)^3}} = \sqrt{\frac{8.0 \times 10^{14}}{2.39 \times 10^{14}}}$
$= \sqrt{3.347} = 1.83$ T.

subject: Physics
q: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>35</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> travels in free space along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-direction. At a particular point (in space and time) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of magnetic field at this point is:
question: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>35</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> travels in free space along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-direction. At a particular point (in space and time) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of magnetic field at this point is:
solution:
At every instant, the ratio of the magnitude of the electric field to the magnitude of the magnetic field in an electromagnetic wave equals the speed of light.

Therefore, EB=c

⇒EB=3×108

⇒B=E3×108=9.63×108

⇒B=3.2×10-8 T

Now, direction of magnetic field can be calculated as

B^=v^×E^

=i^×j^=k^

So, B→=3.2×10-8k^T

q: An object is placed in a medium of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>. An electromagnetic wave of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> falls normally on the object and it is absorbed completely. The radiation pressure on the object would be (speed of light in free space <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ):
question: An object is placed in a medium of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>. An electromagnetic wave of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> falls normally on the object and it is absorbed completely. The radiation pressure on the object would be (speed of light in free space <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ):
solution:
The formula to calculate the radiation pressure is given by

P=Iv   ...1

Also, the velocity of the electromagnetic wave can be expressed as

μ=cv   ...2

Equations (1) and (2) imply that

P=I·μc=6×108×33×108=6 N m-2

subject: Physics
q: A point light source emits E.M. waves in free space. A detector, placed at a distance of $L$ m, measures the intensity as $I_o$. The detector is now shifted to another location on the same spherical surface ensuring the angle between original location and new location as $45°$. The measured intensity at new location will be _______.
question: A point light source emits E.M. waves in free space. A detector, placed at a distance of $L$ m, measures the intensity as $I_o$. The detector is now shifted to another location on the same spherical surface ensuring the angle between original location and new location as $45°$. The measured intensity at new location will be _______.
solution:
For a point light source, the intensity of electromagnetic waves at a distance $r$ is given by $I = \dfrac{P}{4\pi r^2}$, where $P$ is the power of the source.

Since the detector is moved to a new location on the same spherical surface, the distance from the source remains $L$.

The emission from a point source is isotropic, meaning the intensity depends only on the radial distance from the source and is independent of the angular position.

Therefore, the intensity at the new location remains unchanged, which is $I_o$.

Answer: $I_o$

subject: Physics
q: A monochromatic beam of light has a frequency <math><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math> and is propagating along the direction <math><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac></math> . It is polarized along the <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> direction. The acceptance form the magnetic field is:
question: A monochromatic beam of light has a frequency <math><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math> and is propagating along the direction <math><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac></math> . It is polarized along the <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> direction. The acceptance form the magnetic field is:
solution:
E^×B^ should have given direction of wave propagation,⇒  K^×B^∥ i^+j^ 2 
 K^×i^+j^ 2=j^--i^2=i^+j^ 2 ∥i^+j^ 2  . 

Wave propagation vector K^ should be along i^+j^ 2.

q: There are two infinitely long straight current-carrying conductors and they are held at right angles to each other so that their common ends meet at the origin as shown in the figure given below. The ratio of current in both conductors is 1:1. The magnetic field at point P is_________.
question: There are two infinitely long straight current-carrying conductors and they are held at right angles to each other so that their common ends meet at the origin as shown in the figure given below. The ratio of current in both conductors is 1:1. The magnetic field at point P is_________.
solution:
B=μ0I4πy1+sinθ1+μ0I4πx1+sinθ2
B=μ0I4πy1+xx2+y2+μ0I4πx1+yx2+y2

B=μ0I4πx+μ0I4πy+μ0I4πxyx2+y2+yxx2+y2⇒B=μ0I4π1x+1y+μ0I4πx2+y2xy=μ0I4πx+y+x2+y2xy
